audit_status.source_base: Hebrew 1 Samuel 13, first-pass restored draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: Reviewed for damaged regnal formula, Jonathan/Saul credit, troop and chariot numbers, civilian hiding, Gilgal timing, unauthorized offering interpretation, dynasty rejection, heart-language misuse, Philistine metallurgy control, and under-resourcing safeguards.

verses.restored: Saul was a son of a year in his reigning, and he reigned two years over Israel; the Hebrew numbers are damaged or incomplete.

Saul chose for himself three thousand from Israel: two thousand were with Saul at Michmash and hill country of Bethel, and one thousand were with Jonathan at Gibeah of Benjamin. He sent rest of people away, each to tent.
Jonathan struck garrison of Philistines that was at Geba, and Philistines heard. Saul blew trumpet throughout land, saying, Let Hebrews hear.
All Israel heard it said, Saul has struck garrison of Philistines, and Israel has also become odious to Philistines. People were summoned after Saul to Gilgal.

Philistines assembled to fight with Israel: thirty thousand chariots according to Hebrew text, six thousand horsemen, and people like sand upon shore in multitude. They went up and camped at Michmash, east of Beth-aven.
Men of Israel saw they were in distress, for people were hard pressed. People hid themselves in caves, thickets, rocks, pits, and cisterns.
Hebrews crossed Jordan to land of Gad and Gilead. Saul was still at Gilgal, and all people following him trembled.
He waited seven days, according to appointed time Samuel had set, but Samuel did not come to Gilgal, and people scattered from him.
Saul said, Bring near to me ascending offering and peace offerings. He offered ascending offering.
It happened as he finished offering ascending offering, look, Samuel came. Saul went out to meet him and bless/greet him.
Samuel said, What have you done? Saul said, Because I saw people scattering from me, you did not come within appointed days, and Philistines were assembling at Michmash,
I said, Now Philistines will come down against me at Gilgal, and face of YHWH I have not entreated. So I compelled myself and offered ascending offering.
Samuel said to Saul, You have acted foolishly. You have not kept commandment of YHWH your Divine that he commanded you, for now YHWH would have established your kingdom over Israel to age.

But now your kingdom shall not endure. YHWH has sought for himself a man according to his heart, and YHWH has commanded him as leader over his people, because you did not keep what YHWH commanded you.
Samuel arose and went up from Gilgal to Gibeah of Benjamin. Saul mustered people found with him, about six hundred men.
Saul, Jonathan his son, and people found with them stayed in Geba of Benjamin, while Philistines camped at Michmash.
Destroying parties came out from camp of Philistines in three companies. One company turned toward Ophrah, toward land of Shual;
one company turned toward Beth-horon; and one company turned toward border overlooking valley of Zeboim toward wilderness.
No smith was found in all land of Israel, for Philistines said, Lest Hebrews make sword or spear.
All Israel went down to Philistines, each to sharpen his plowshare, mattock, axe, and sickle.
The charge was a pim for plowshares and mattocks, and a third of shekel for sharpening axes and setting goads, according to difficult Hebrew.
So it happened on day of battle that neither sword nor spear was found in hand of any people with Saul and Jonathan, but they were found with Saul and Jonathan his son.
A Philistine outpost went out toward pass of Michmash.

translation_notes: Verse 1 is textually damaged; confident age and reign-length claims exceed the preserved Hebrew.
Jonathan initiates the attack, while public messaging credits Saul. The chapter exposes how royal narratives can absorb subordinates' actions.
Military figures, especially thirty thousand chariots, require witness and historical audit.
People hiding and crossing the Jordan are civilians and soldiers responding to overwhelming danger, not cowards.
Saul acts under panic, delay, troop desertion, and strategic threat. Context explains his decision without making office or fear exempt from accountability.
Samuel's condemnation has dynastic and theological force but must not be used to punish anxiety, imperfect ritual, or lay spiritual practice.
'A man according to his heart' concerns the sought leader's commission and cannot establish moral perfection or immunity for David.
Philistine control of metalwork is economic and military domination. Technology restriction and debt-like sharpening costs deepen dependency.
Israel's lack of weapons does not make under-equipped people spiritually superior or justify sending them into preventable danger.
safety_note: Do not weaponize Saul's panic to shame anxious people, condemn non-clergy for prayer or ritual, or demand obedience to absent leaders. Do not romanticize under-equipment, military escalation, or Jonathan's attack. Damaged numbers and difficult economic terms must remain marked as uncertain.
